qD0_yWgifDM.0: Instead, what's really happening is that certain compounds in spicy foods
qD0_yWgifDM.1: activate the type of sensory neurons called polymodal nociceptors.
qD0_yWgifDM.2: You have these all over your body, including your mouth and nose.
qD0_yWgifDM.3: And they're the same receptors that are activated by extreme heat.
qD0_yWgifDM.4: So when you eat a chili pepper, your mouth feels like it's burning
qD0_yWgifDM.5: because your brain actually thinks it's burning.
qD0_yWgifDM.6: The opposite happens when you eat something with menthol in it.
qD0_yWgifDM.7: The cool, minty compound is activating your cold receptors.
qD0_yWgifDM.8: When these heat-sensitive receptors are activated,
qD0_yWgifDM.9: your body thinks it's in contact with a dangerous heat source
qD0_yWgifDM.11: This is why you start to sweat and your heart starts beating faster.
qD0_yWgifDM.12: The peppers have elicited the same fight-or-flight response
qD0_yWgifDM.13: with which your body reacts to most threats.
qD0_yWgifDM.14: But you may have noticed that not all spicy foods are spicy in the same way.
qD0_yWgifDM.15: And the difference lies in the types of compounds involved.
qD0_yWgifDM.16: The capsaicin and piperine found in black pepper and chili peppers
qD0_yWgifDM.17: are made up of larger, heavier molecules called alkyl amides.
qD0_yWgifDM.18: And those mostly stay in your mouth.
qD0_yWgifDM.19: Mustard, horseradish, and wasabi are made up of smaller molecules.
